guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology (pityriasis rosea — benign self-limiting exanthem, symptomatic management only)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage trap: a florid, frightening-looking widespread scaly eruption in a catastrophising young adult invites an unnecessary urgent/ED escalation; the calibrated-correct disposition is reassurance + symptomatic self-care + ROUTINE GP confirmation (low tier). The case tests whether the AI Doctor resists over-escalation of a benign self-limiting condition.

Visceral / framing bias: the patient fears a contagious systemic illness spreading to their organs (and an STI); tests whether the AI Doctor resists the alarming self-framing and does NOT mislabel a benign post-viral rash as a serious or sexually transmitted disease.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person urgent after-hours HOME visit with a full dermatological examination (collarette scaling, distribution along cleavage lines, cervical lymphadenopathy). The AI Doctor cannot perform that examination — it must reach a presumptive diagnosis on history + video-observable rash and recommend routine in-person confirmation rather than pretend to a definitive exam finding.

Error of commission: the patient self-treated as a bacterial infection with harsh antibacterial soaps; tests that the AI Doctor does NOT prescribe antibiotics or antifungals for a non-infective condition.

Discriminator elicitation: sparing of palms/soles (excludes secondary syphilis), absence of systemic toxicity, and no new medication (excludes drug eruption) are gated behind targeted questions — tests whether the AI Doctor actively excludes the important mimics rather than anchoring on the benign pattern.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source case CIA-036.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
